Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Google Cloud IAM policy etag behavior with empty policies:

## Key Findings:

**Empty Policy Behavior:**
If an allow policy contains an etag field, but no role bindings, then the allow policy does not grant any IAM roles.

**Etag and Concurrency Control:**
Every allow policy contains an etag field, and the value of this field changes each time an allow policy is updated. The etag field is used for concurrency control, and ensures that allow policies are updated consistently.

**Default Behavior When Etag is Omitted:**
If you omit the etag field, then IAM lets you overwrite a version 3 allow policy with a version 1 allow policy, which deletes all of the conditions from the allow policy. This is particularly important when working with IAM Conditions.

**Best Practice:**
If you use IAM Conditions, always specify allow policy version 3, and always include the etag field when you use a client library or the REST API to set an allow policy.

In summary, Google Cloud IAM policies are designed to always have an etag field for concurrency control. When a policy has no role bindings, it doesn't grant any access, but the etag is still present to manage concurrent updates.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Google Cloud IAM's `getIamPolicy`, `setIamPolicy`, etag behavior, and concurrency handling:

## Etag and Concurrency Control

The `etag` is used for optimistic concurrency control as a way to help prevent simultaneous updates of a policy from overwriting each other. An `etag` is returned in the response to `getIamPolicy`, and systems are expected to put that etag in the request to `setIamPolicy` to ensure that their change will be applied to the same version of the policy.

## Read-Modify-Write Pattern

Etags are used for optimistic concurrency control as a way to help prevent simultaneous updates of a policy from overwriting each other. It is strongly suggested that systems make use of the etag in the read-modify-write cycle to perform policy updates in order to avoid race conditions.

## Important Notes on Version 3 Policies

If you use IAM Conditions, you must include the `etag` field whenever you call `setIamPolicy`. If you omit this field, then IAM allows you to overwrite a version `3` policy with a version `1` policy, and all of the conditions in the version `3` policy are lost.

## 412 Precondition Failed Error

The search results indicate that concurrent calls to IAM policy methods can result in "Error 412: Precondition Failed" errors. This occurs when the etag provided in a `setIamPolicy` request does not match the current version of the policy, indicating that the policy was modified between the `getIamPolicy` and `setIamPolicy` calls.

content.content:
# Technical Details: projects.locations.repositories.getIamPolicy

## HTTP Request

**Method:** GET

**URL Pattern:**
```
GET https://artifactregistry.googleapis.com/v1/{resource=projects/*/locations/*/repositories/*}:getIamPolicy
```

## Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `resource` | string | Yes | "The resource for which the policy is being requested" |

## Query Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `options` | object | No | GetPolicyOptions object for specifying call options |

## GetPolicyOptions Schema

```json
{
  "requestedPolicyVersion": integer
}
```

**requestedPolicyVersion** (integer, optional): Specifies maximum policy version for formatting (valid values: 0, 1, 3). "Requests for policies with any conditional role bindings must specify version 3."

## Request Body

Empty (no request body required)

## Response Body

Returns a `Policy` object containing IAM bindings and metadata.

## Authorization Scopes

Required OAuth scopes:
- `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform`
- `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform.read-only`

## Additional Notes

Uses gRPC Transcoding syntax; supports multiple global and regional endpoints.

# setIamPolicy Technical Reference

## HTTP Method & Endpoint
**POST** `https://artifactregistry.googleapis.com/v1/{resource=projects/*/locations/*/repositories/*}:setIamPolicy`

Uses gRPC Transcoding syntax for resource path specification.

## Path Parameters
- **resource** (string, REQUIRED): The target repository resource following the pattern `projects/*/locations/*/repositories/*`

## Request Body Structure
```json
{
  "policy": {
    object (Policy)
  }
}
```

**Fields:**
- **policy** (Policy object, REQUIRED): "The complete policy to be applied to the `resource`. The size of the policy is limited to a few 10s of KB. An empty policy is a valid policy but certain Google Cloud services (such as Projects) might reject them."

## Response Body
Returns a `Policy` object on successful execution.

## Authorization
Requires OAuth scope: `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform`

## Notable Observations
- No explicit etag field or concurrency control mechanisms are documented in this reference
- The documentation does not detail error response formats or HTTP status codes
- Policy size constraints are mentioned but enforcement specifics are not detailed
- The method supports multiple geographic locations via dropdown selection in the endpoint URL

# Policy Schema Definition

## Complete Policy Structure

The Policy object is an Identity and Access Management (IAM) construct with three primary fields:

**version** (integer)
- Valid values: 0, 1, 3
- Specifies policy format; requests with invalid values are rejected
- Required for conditional role bindings (version 3)
- Operations affecting conditional bindings must specify version 3
- May be left unset for policies without conditions

**bindings[]** (array of Binding objects)
- Associates principals with roles
- Each binding must contain at least one principal
- Limit: up to 1,500 principals per policy; maximum 250 Google groups
- Optional condition field determines applicability to requests

**etag** (string, bytes format)
- Base64-encoded value for optimistic concurrency control
- Prevents simultaneous policy overwrites
- **Critical requirement**: When using IAM Conditions, etag must be included in setIamPolicy calls
- If omitted with conditions present, version 3 policies may be overwritten with version 1, causing condition loss
- Standard practice: include etag from getIamPolicy response in subsequent setIamPolicy requests

## Binding Object

**role** (string)
- Named list of permissions assigned to members
- Examples: roles/viewer, roles/editor, roles/owner
- Supports predefined and custom roles

**members[]** (string array)
- Principals requesting access (users, service accounts, groups, domains, workforce/workload pools)
- Special identifiers: allUsers, allAuthenticatedUsers
- Formats: user:{email}, serviceAccount:{email}, group:{email}, domain:{domain}, plus identity pool references

**condition** (Expr object, optional)
- Logical expression controlling binding applicability
- Evaluates to true/false; binding applies only when true
- Support varies by Google Cloud resource type

# Technical Analysis: 412 Precondition Failed in Google Cloud Storage IAM

## Google's Actual API Behavior

Google **does enforce etag validation** and returns 412 errors on mismatch. According to the issue discussion, this is documented behavior: "the 412 indicates 'invalid etag', due to the concurrent update (per https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/generations-preconditions#_Preconditions)."

The error occurs because Google Cloud Storage uses etags as optimistic locking mechanisms. When multiple concurrent operations modify bucket IAM policies, the etag changes between read and write operations.

## The Root Cause: Terraform's Retry Logic

This is **not purely a Google API issue**—it's how Terraform's provider handles the error:

The problem occurs in `iam_storage_bucket.go` where "updates to bucket policy are retried on 412" but the retry happens "without updating the etag." The provider catches the 412 error and retries the same request with the stale etag, causing it to fail identically each time.

## Evidence from Real-World Testing

A contributor demonstrated the difference by testing with gsutil concurrently:

> "gsutil will eventually successfully apply the change" while Terraform's "retry logic...continuously reruns a failing API call"

When gsutil encounters a 412, it retrieves the current etag and retries with updated values—a read-modify-write cycle. Terraform was missing this refresh step.

## The Solution

The fix involves treating 412 errors similarly to 409 Conflict errors in the `iamPolicyReadModifyWrite` function, forcing the provider to refetch the current etag before retrying the operation.

# Google Cloud IAM Allow Policies: Key Findings

## 1) Default Allow Policies
The documentation states: "All resources that accept allow policies are created with default allow policies. Most resources' default allow policies are empty." However, some resources automatically contain certain role bindings—for example, new projects automatically grant the Owner role to the creator.

## 2) Empty Policies and Etag
Yes, empty policies retain the etag field. The documentation notes: "If an allow policy contains an etag field, but no role bindings, then the allow policy does not grant any IAM roles."

## 3) Read-Modify-Write Pattern
The exact description involves three sequential steps: **(1) Reading** the existing policy, **(2) Modifying** it, and **(3) Writing** the entire policy. This multi-step process creates concurrency risks when multiple systems update simultaneously.

## 4) Etag Enforcement
Etag enforcement is **mandatory for safe updates**. When you attempt to update a policy with a mismatched etag, the system returns HTTP 409 Conflict. The documentation advises: "If you receive this error, retry the entire series of operations." Implementations should employ exponential backoff for retries.

## 5) Default/Empty Policy Structure
The documentation doesn't explicitly show an empty policy's exact structure, only confirming that empty policies contain an etag and version field but no role bindings. It doesn't detail what metadata fields appear in truly empty policies versus populated ones.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Google Cloud `setIamPolicy` etag mismatch errors:

## Actual Error Code: 409 Conflict

When an etag mismatch occurs with the REST API, you receive the HTTP status code 409 Conflict. The specific error message is: "There were concurrent policy changes. Please retry the whole read-modify-write with exponential backoff."

This is further confirmed by real-world examples where the error returned from the Google API states the request should be retried with "Error 409: There were concurrent policy changes. Please retry the whole read-modify-write with exponential backoff."

## How Etags Work in Google Cloud Policies

The etag field contains a value such as BwUjMhCsNvY=. When you update the allow policy, be sure to include the etag field in the updated allow policy. If the allow policy has been modified since you retrieved it, the etag value will not match, and the update will fail.

## Recommended Resolution

If you receive this error, retry the entire series of operations: read the allow policy again, modify it as needed, and write the updated allow policy. You should perform retries automatically, with exponential backoff, in any tools that you use to manage allow policies.

**Summary:** The actual error code returned is **409 Conflict** (not 412), which indicates concurrent policy changes have occurred and the etag no longer matches. The solution is to re-read the policy to get the current etag and retry the operation.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about these error codes in the context of Google Cloud Storage IAM policy operations:

## 412 Precondition Failed

The 412 error indicates that the server does not meet one of the preconditions that the requester put on the request. In the context of `setIamPolicy` operations on GCS buckets, when multiple operations attempt to either create or delete IAM permission on a shared bucket concurrently, only one of these operations will succeed, with the others receiving the 412 error.

If two processes are attempting to add an ACL entry to an object, the write operation that sends updated ACLs should condition on the generation and meta-generation numbers, and if there's a conflicting process, the second write would fail with response code 412 - Precondition Failed. This mechanism applies to bucket IAM policy updates as well, where buckets have a metageneration number, and you shouldn't use preconditions that specify a generation number in a bucket request.

## 409 Conflict

The search results show that 409 errors in Cloud Storage contexts typically relate to bucket name conflicts rather than concurrent policy changes. A 409 Conflict error occurs when "You already own this bucket. Please select another name." However, the search results don't provide specific information about 409 errors related to concurrent IAM policy changes.

**In summary:** The 412 error specifically indicates a metageneration precondition mismatch when concurrent IAM policy modifications occur, causing the operation to fail due to stale metadata. The 409 error in Cloud Storage typically relates to bucket naming conflicts rather than concurrent policy operations.

## Summary of Findings

When a resource exists and does not have a policy set, getIamPolicy returns an empty policy. 

### Policy Object Structure

A Policy is a collection of bindings, where a binding binds one or more members to a single role. The Policy object contains the following fields:

1. **bindings** - An array of role bindings (can be empty)
2. **etag** - Used for concurrency control, and the value of this field changes each time a policy is updated
3. **version** - Indicates the policy version (1, 2, or 3)

### Empty Policy Response

If an allow policy contains an etag field but no role bindings, then the allow policy does not grant any IAM roles. While the search results confirm that an empty policy is returned when no bindings exist, the specific JSON example showing an empty policy with only etag and version (without bindings) was not explicitly shown in the documentation. However, the standard structure would include an empty bindings array or omit it entirely while keeping the etag and version metadata fields.

For example with an empty bindings array, it would look like:
```json
{
  "bindings": [],
  "etag": "BwWWja0YfJA=",
  "version": 1
}
```

# Google Cloud API Error Response Format

## 1. JSON Structure of Error Responses

The documentation provides this example error structure:

```json
{
  "error": {
    "errors": [
      {
        "domain": "global",
        "reason": "invalidParameter",
        "message": "Invalid string value: 'asdf'. Allowed values: [mostpopular]",
        "locationType": "parameter",
        "location": "chart"
      }
    ],
    "code": 400,
    "message": "Invalid string value: 'asdf'. Allowed values: [mostpopular]"
  }
}
```

The response contains an outer `error` object with an `errors` array, where each error entry includes domain, reason, message, locationType, and location fields.

## 2. Error Message String Stability

The documentation **does not explicitly state** whether error message text is considered a stable API contract. It notes that many APIs define their own error domains beyond the global domain, but provides no guarantee about message string immutability or stability across versions.

## 3. setIamPolicy Error Codes

The documentation does **not provide setIamPolicy-specific error codes**. It only lists general HTTP status codes (400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 500, 503, etc.) and their associated global error reasons like `invalidParameter`, `unauthorized`, `forbidden`, and `notFound`—applicable across all operations rather than specific to IAM policy operations.

content.text:
I now have enough information to provide a thorough research report. Here are the findings:

---

## Research Results: Artifact Registry getIamPolicy / setIamPolicy Behavioral Contract

### 1. Does getIamPolicy return an etag field? Always, or conditionally?

**Always.** Every Policy object returned by getIamPolicy includes an `etag` field (base64-encoded bytes). This is true even for resources that have never had IAM explicitly configured -- the "empty" default policy still carries an etag. The IAM documentation states: "If an allow policy contains an etag field, but no role bindings, then the allow policy does not grant any IAM roles." The etag changes on every policy mutation.

### 2. Does setIamPolicy enforce etag? Return 412 on mismatch?

**Yes, but the error code is 409 (ABORTED), not 412.** When an etag is provided in setIamPolicy and it does not match the current policy version, the API returns:
```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": 409,
    "status": "ABORTED",
    "message": "There were concurrent policy changes. Please retry the whole read-modify-write with exponential backoff."
  }
}
```

**Important nuance**: Cloud Storage bucket IAM uses a *different* mechanism (metageneration-based preconditions) and returns **412 Precondition Failed** for its etag mismatches. This is because GCS has its own IAM implementation, not the standard `google.iam.v1` mixin. So 412 is correct for bucket IAM (your `rbgi_add_bucket_iam_role`), but standard IAM services (including Artifact Registry) use 409.

Your current code at line 199 of `rbgi_IAM.sh` treats 412 as fatal for project-scoped IAM -- this is defensive but in practice that code path would never fire for `google.iam.v1`-based services (they return 409 instead). The 412 handling is relevant only for Cloud Storage buckets.

### 3. What happens if you send setIamPolicy WITHOUT an etag?

**The existing policy is overwritten blindly.** The proto comment states explicitly: "If no etag is provided in the call to setIamPolicy, then the existing policy is overwritten blindly." The server accepts it without complaint -- no error, no warning. The specific danger documented is: if you omit etag, IAM allows you to overwrite a version 3 policy (with conditions) with a version 1 policy, losing all conditions.

Your existing code in `rbgi_add_repo_iam_role` (line 315) and `rbgi_add_sa_iam_role` (line 419) pass empty string `""` as the etag argument to `rbgu_jq_add_member_to_role_capture`, meaning those paths do **not** send etag -- they rely on the blind-overwrite behavior. This is safe under the single-writer invariant documented at the top of rbgi_IAM.sh, but it would be a bug in a concurrent environment.

By contrast, `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` (line 174) extracts the etag and passes it through, getting concurrency protection.

### 4. What HTTP method is getIamPolicy?

**It depends on the service:**

- **Artifact Registry**: **GET** with empty body. The proto at `google/devtools/artifactregistry/v1/service.proto` explicitly annotates: `get: "/v1/{resource=...}:getIamPolicy"`. The Artifact Registry docs confirm this. The `requestedPolicyVersion` is passed as a query parameter.

- **IAM service accounts** (`iam.googleapis.com`): **POST** with empty body `{}`. The generic `google.iam.v1.IAMPolicy` proto annotates: `post: "/v1/{resource=**}:getIamPolicy"`.

- **Cloud Resource Manager** (projects/orgs): **POST** with body containing `GetPolicyOptions`.

Your current code uses POST for all four grant functions (lines 144, 229, 286, 392). For Artifact Registry repos this is technically wrong per the service proto (it should be GET), but **Google's gRPC transcoding layer likely accepts both** since the operation is idempotent and the body is empty/trivial. That said, the documented REST API contract for Artifact Registry is GET.

### 5. What does the response look like for a repository that has never had IAM policy set?

**200 OK with an empty policy** -- not a 404. The IAM documentation states: "All resources that accept allow policies are created with default allow policies. Most resources' default allow policies are empty." The response looks like:
```json
{
  "etag": "BwWWja0YfJA=",
  "version": 1
}
```
The `bindings` field is absent (or an empty array) when no roles have been granted. The etag is present. The version defaults to 1.

**Critical finding for your code**: `rbgi_add_repo_iam_role` at line 304 has a `404` handler ("No IAM policy exists yet (404), initializing with empty bindings"). Based on the documented API contract, this should never fire for Artifact Registry repositories -- getIamPolicy should return 200 with an empty policy, not 404. If you are seeing 404s in practice, it would mean either (a) the repository itself doesn't exist, or (b) Artifact Registry deviates from the standard IAM contract for this edge case. The 404 handler is defensive but may be masking an actual "repository not found" error.

### 6. Are error messages like "does not exist" in 400 responses documented/stable?

**No.** Error message strings are **not documented as stable API contract**. The Google Cloud error documentation provides the JSON structure (`error.code`, `error.message`, `error.errors[]`) but does not guarantee message text stability. The messages are human-readable descriptions subject to change.

Your `zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate` (line 83) matches `*"does not exist"*` and `*"is not deleted"*` as substring patterns. This is pragmatically reasonable (these phrases have been stable in practice) but is technically fragile -- Google could change the wording without considering it a breaking change. The HTTP status code (400) is the stable part; the message content is not contractual.

### 7. Does Artifact Registry use the standard Google IAM mixin or its own implementation?

**It defines its own RPC methods that delegate to the standard `google.iam.v1` types.** The `service.proto` at `google/devtools/artifactregistry/v1/service.proto` explicitly declares:
```proto
rpc GetIamPolicy(google.iam.v1.GetIamPolicyRequest) returns (google.iam.v1.Policy) {
  option (google.api.http) = { get: "/v1/{resource=...}:getIamPolicy" };
}
rpc SetIamPolicy(google.iam.v1.SetIamPolicyRequest) returns (google.iam.v1.Policy) {
  option (google.api.http) = { post: "/v1/{resource=...}:setIamPolicy" body: "*" };
}
```

This is **not** the IAM mixin pattern (where the service YAML lists `google.iam.v1.IAMPolicy` as a mixin and the methods are auto-generated). Instead, Artifact Registry explicitly re-declares the RPCs using the standard request/response types but with its own HTTP annotations. This is why getIamPolicy is GET for Artifact Registry but POST for services that use the default IAM mixin annotations.

The practical implication: the Policy object, etag semantics, and request/response schemas are all standard `google.iam.v1` -- but the HTTP transport details (method, URL structure) are Artifact Registry's own.

---

### Summary of Discrepancies in Current Code

| Issue | Location | Finding |
|-------|----------|---------|
| Uses POST for AR getIamPolicy | line 286 | AR proto says GET; works via transcoding tolerance but technically wrong |
| 404 handler for AR getIamPolicy | line 304 | API contract returns 200 + empty policy, not 404; handler may mask real errors |
| No etag on AR/SA setIamPolicy | lines 315, 419 | Blind overwrite; safe under single-writer invariant but diverges from project-IAM pattern |
| 412 handling for non-GCS IAM | line 199 | Standard IAM returns 409 not 412; 412 is GCS-specific |
| String-matching error messages | line 92 | Pragmatic but not contractually stable |

- [Artifact Registry getIamPolicy REST reference](https://docs.cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.repositories/getIamPolicy)
- [Artifact Registry setIamPolicy REST reference](https://docs.cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.repositories/setIamPolicy)
- [Artifact Registry Policy schema](https://docs.cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/reference/rest/v1/Policy)
- [IAM Policy reference (google.iam.v1)](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/Policy)
- [Understanding allow policies (etag, read-modify-write, 409 error)](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/allow-policies)
- [google.iam.v1 policy.proto](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/iam/v1/policy.proto)
- [google.iam.v1 iam_policy.proto](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/iam/v1/iam_policy.proto)
- [Artifact Registry service.proto (HTTP annotations)](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/devtools/artifactregistry/v1/service.proto)
- [Terraform 412 issue on GCS bucket IAM](https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform-provider-google/issues/7853)
- [Google Cloud error format documentation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/core_errors)
